name: Jimmy Nottingham
abstract: James Edward Nottingham, Jr. (December 15, 1925 – November 16, 1978), also known as Sir James, was an American jazz trumpeter and flugelhorn player.

concerts.label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Savoy Ballroom "The Home of Happy Feet"

works.t: When the Lights Go On Again (All Over the World)

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: That was a band that included Clarke Terry and Jimmy Nottingham... Then we got Jimmy Nottingham.

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: I think Phil Woods and Chris Woods were there, weren’t they? Jimmy Nottingham. We had Richard Williams at one time. We’ve had a lot of wailing cats in that band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Temperley
about_quotes.evidence: When I actually played with the Thad Jones and Mel Lewis Band... looking at Snooky Young and Jimmy Nottingham... all these people that were in the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Temperley interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Gerald Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Jimmy Nottingham, you know Jimmy Nottingham? Basie’s got a powerful band. Then we went in and we recorded for Victor... So Snooky didn’t come back but Clark Terry came in. Jimmy Nottingham, you know Jimmy Nottingham?
about_quotes.doc_title: Gerald Wilson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Snooky Young and Gerald Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Interviewer mentions Jimmy Nottingham as being at Great Lakes; Interviewee does not claim personal interaction, only notes the band started there.
about_quotes.doc_title: Snooky Young and Gerald Wilson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Jones Hank
about_quotes.evidence: I mentioned some names like Jimmy Nottingham and Joe Waller... Thad, Jimmy Nottingham, myself. There were very few.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jones Hank — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
